During the monitoring of the candy packaging line, it was verified that the weight, packaging, and palletizing parameters fell within established specifications. However, significant deviations in product appearance were identified, specifically unwrapped candies sticking to one another. It is recommended to review and reinforce continuous monitoring to ensure the final product is of optimal quality.

Thanks Alan. So should we count those 2 pallets of accumulation as part of our total numbers on the shift report?

Good afternoon Alex Mendoza, Brandon Lance, Gustavo Rodas, Dave Ruff, Hervin Herrera
 
With the issues we are seeing on the Truffle line related to product being blown off, please ensure Maintenance is consistently on the line and actively monitoring performance. As soon as we reach two layers of accumulation on a pallet, stop the line and refocus on the process. Our goal is to capture every piece we make and receive full credit for the work being done.
 
For example, 1st shift reported producing 188,750 pieces with a 62% attainment, while also accumulating approximately two pallets of unwrapped product, which equates to roughly 68,000 pieces. If those accumulated pieces are included, the shift would have actually produced approximately 256,750 pieces, resulting in an attainment of approximately 84%.
 
We must continue improving the process as we run the line and document any changes we make. Capturing what is working—and what is not—will help us start future runs in a much better position.
 
I spoke with Rose Smuin, and we have a path forward for the accumulated Truffles. The product we have already made can be sent to L3, where it will be wrapped on the Cute Cavanna during 3rd shift tomorrow. Hervin Herrera will create the rework packets needed to support this effort. Please ensure that every completed pallet of unwrapped product is properly labeled so we maintain full traceability when transferring material to L3.
 
That said, this is not a permanent solution, nor does it mean we can become comfortable with accumulation. We still need to focus on improving line performance, reducing blow-offs, and eliminating the need for rework whenever possible.
Let's stay focused on making the process better, capturing all production, and setting ourselves up for long-term success.
 
Chad Stone, Maria Moron Quintero, Daniel Bringas, Amy Hadrich
Not right now. Let me talk with Amy Hadrich and see how we should handle it.
Are there labels being created in NetSuite for the 2 pallets of accumulation?
Amy Hadrich, yes, they are creating labels. There should be 5 made for the pallets from the start of production.

S2 is down to clean the drive roller on tunnel 3 it keeps slipping

We removed and cleaned the rollers and scrapers by the drive roller. 

Line resumed 5:10PM.

After resuming the line; the belt will run, then stop. 

So we’re pausing again for 10 minutes to clear all the salt build up underneath the belt.

There was a lot of salt accumulated under the belts. After removing the salt we’re still seeing the belt slip. 

So, we’ve loosened the tension and swept the recently accumulated salt from the drive roller and scrapers. 
And are now turning on the belt washer to see if that helps with the slipping.
Started at the Enrober again at 6:05PM. The belt is running with little to no slippage.

We have had to pause operations several times due to excess salt buildup on the Tunnel 3 belts; this caused the belt to slow down, resulting in product pieces sticking together in groups of 2 to 5. We have cleaned the tunnel and removed the salt, as well as removed the stuck pieces—which had to be discarded as scrap. We are now noticing that in the caramel depositor, the droplet is trailing a line of caramel down the center of the belt, which continues to cause pieces to stick together. The operator has made some temperature adjustments, but pending further changes, I have informed the supervisor that we need someone to sort the pieces.
Thank you Alfonsina Gil

The changes made at the depositor and tunnel 2 belt have the drops adequately separated. 

We’ll continue to observe for 10(min)that there is a consistent separation between the pieces, after which we’ll rotate the EE separating pieces back to the pack area.
We need to improve the M7 printer; it is illegible. I informed the line leader, and I will notify the supervisor.

End of 1 shift
S2 is down to clean the drive roller on tunnel 3 it keeps slipping

We removed and cleaned the rollers and scrapers by the drive roller. 

Line resumed 5:10PM.

After resuming the line; the belt will run, then stop. 

So we’re pausing again for 10 minutes to clear all the salt build up underneath the belt.

There was a lot of salt accumulated under the belts. After removing the salt we’re still seeing the belt slip. 

So, we’ve loosened the tension and swept the recently accumulated salt from the drive roller and scrapers. 
And are now turning on the belt washer to see if that helps with the slipping.
Started at the Enrober again at 6:05PM. The belt is running with little to no slippage.

We have had to pause operations several times due to excess salt buildup on the Tunnel 3 belts; this caused the belt to slow down, resulting in product pieces sticking together in groups of 2 to 5. We have cleaned the tunnel and removed the salt, as well as removed the stuck pieces—which had to be discarded as scrap. We are now noticing that in the caramel depositor, the droplet is trailing a line of caramel down the center of the belt, which continues to cause pieces to stick together. The operator has made some temperature adjustments, but pending further changes, I have informed the supervisor that we need someone to sort the pieces.
Thank you Alfonsina Gil

The changes made at the depositor and tunnel 2 belt have the drops adequately separated. 

We’ll continue to observe for 10(min)that there is a consistent separation between the pieces, after which we’ll rotate the EE separating pieces back to the pack area.
